result:
# The Weight of Glass

The coffee shop closes in twenty minutes and I'm watching a woman at the corner table rebuild her entire life on a laptop screen. She doesn't know I'm watching—I'm not, really, not in any invasive way. It's more that she exists so loudly in her concentration that the whole room bends toward her.

Her fingers pause. She deletes something. Types it again. Deletes it again.

I know that rhythm.

---

There's something nobody tells you about getting older: time doesn't speed up uniformly. Tuesday afternoons still crawl. The hour before a flight still stretches into taffy. What changes is the *looking back*. You turn around and five years are just sitting there, collapsed into a single feeling, a song, the smell of a specific laundry detergent you don't use anymore.

I've been thinking about glass lately. How it's technically a liquid, always flowing, just too slowly for us to perceive. How the windows in ancient cathedrals are thicker at the bottom—though I read somewhere this is a myth, just uneven manufacturing, and now I don't know what to believe.

Maybe it doesn't matter. The poetry of it is still true: everything flows. Everything is in the process of becoming something else. We just can't see it happening.

---

My grandmother used to say that worry was interest paid on trouble that never comes due. She also used to hide cash in her freezer, label everything in the refrigerator with masking tape, and call every Thursday at exactly 6:15 PM until she couldn't anymore.

The last time I saw her fully herself, she was teaching me to make pierogi. Her hands knew the dough in a way her words couldn't explain. "Not like that," she kept saying. "Like *this*." And her fingers would move, and I would try to copy them, and she would shake her head, and we'd do it again.

I still can't make them right. The dough tears. The edges don't seal. Something about the pressure, the speed, the particular way she'd pinch and turn—it lived in her body and died there too.

Maybe that's what we really lose when we lose people. Not just their presence but their specific knowledge, the things they knew without knowing they knew them.

---

The woman at the corner table just closed her laptop. She's staring out the window now, at the parking lot, at nothing. Her coffee went cold an hour ago. 

I want to tell her something. That it gets easier, or harder, or different. That whatever she's rebuilding will hold. That the document she keeps rewriting will eventually say what she means.

But I don't know any of that. And strangers don't want your hope. They want to be left alone with their own.

---

Here's what I've learned, or think I've learned, or am learning:

The things that save us are usually small. Not revelations—those are too bright, too brief. What saves us is the ordinary things we return to. The way the light comes in through a particular window. The first sip of water when you're actually thirsty. The moment just before a song you love begins, when you recognize the opening notes and your whole body knows what's coming.

We're so hungry for transformation, for the dramatic turn, the montage, the moment everything changes. But change mostly happens like glass flows—imperceptibly, over decades, until one day you look and the window is a different shape entirely.

---

I used to keep lists of things I wanted to accomplish. I'd write them on January 1st with great ceremony, review them in June, abandon them by September. Now I just try to pay attention. That's the whole list: *Pay attention*.

It's harder than it sounds. The world is designed to scatter your focus, to pull you toward the next thing and the next thing and the next thing. Attention has become the rarest commodity, and we give it away so cheaply, to people who don't want it for our benefit.

But when you actually pay attention—to the texture of bread, to the exact color of the sky at 5:47 PM, to the way someone's voice changes when they're trying not to cry—the world gets larger. Not metaphorically. Literally. There's more of it than you thought.

---

The barista is wiping down tables, working his way toward us. The woman at the corner table is packing up her things. The moment is ending.

They all do. That's not sad—or rather, it's sad the way autumn is sad, the way minor keys are sad. There's beauty in the ending of things. There's relief in it too.

I don't know what she was working on. A resignation letter, a novel, a message to someone she used to love. I don't know if she finished it or if she'll open it again tomorrow and start over. I don't know anything about her except that she was here, in this coffee shop, on this Tuesday, concentrating so hard that I could feel it from across the room.

That's enough. That's something. 

The glass keeps flowing. The windows keep changing shape. And we keep showing up to places that are about to close, trying to get the words right before they turn off the lights.

---

*The barista says five minutes. The woman leaves without looking back. I close my notebook, or I would, if this were a notebook and not whatever it actually is—a thought, a moment, a small thing offered to the silence.*
